--- Page 1 ---
arXiv:2502.05906v1  [cs.GT]  9 Feb 2025
Strategic Queues with Priority Classes
Maurizio D’Andrea and Marco Scarsini
Dipartimento di Economia e Finanza, Luiss University, Viale Romania 32,
00197 Roma, Italy
February 11, 2025
Abstract
We consider a strategic M/M/1 queueing model under a ﬁrst-come ﬁrst-served
regime, where customers are split into two classes and class A has priority over class
B. Customers can decide whether to join the queue or balk, and, in case they have
joined the queue, whether and when to renege. We study the equilibrium strategies
and compare the equilibrium outcome and the social optimum in the two cases where
the social optimum is or is not constrained by priority.
Keywords: M/M/1, ﬁrst-come ﬁrst-served, Markov perfect equilibrium, social opti-
mum, priority classes.
1
Introduction
1.1
The Problem
Many real-life situations involves queues where customers make strategic decisions.
For
instance, they choose which queue to join at the supermarket; they decide at what time to
a restaurant who does not take reservations; they decide when to hang up after being put
on hold when calling a call center; they choose whether to pay for a status that guarantees
priority when boarding planes, etc. The analysis of strategic queueing models started with
Naor (1969), who considered an M/M/1 queue under a ﬁrst-come ﬁrst-served (FCFS) regime,
where customers are homogeneous, receive a ﬁxed reward for being served and pay a ﬁxed
cost for any unit of time spent in the system. Their decision is whether to join the queue or
balk upon arrival. He framed this problem as a game and showed that the unique equilibrium
is given by a threshold strategy, i.e., each customer joins the queue if and only if it is shorter
than a certain threshold, which depends on the parameters of the model. In this model, even
if customers were allowed to renege, they would have no reason to do so.
The homogeneity assumption is fundamental for Naor’s result.
Customers are often
inhomogeneous and can diﬀer in various respects. For instance, they could be split into
1


--- Page 2 ---
classes that are associated with diﬀerent priorities, that is, high-priority customers are always
ahead of low priority-customers in the queue. Therefore, low-priority customers get served
only when there are no high-priority customers in the system. In this case, the equilibrium
of the game is more complicated than in the homogeneous case. In particular, whereas the
high-priority customers are unaﬀected by the presence of low-priority customers and behave
like in the Naor’s model, low-priority customers use a threshold strategy that depends on
the numbers of customers of each type in the system.
1.2
Our Contribution
In this paper we consider an M/M/1 queueing model under a FCFS regime, where customers
belong to either class A or class B. A-customers have priority over B-customers. Within
each class customers are homogeneous. Customers of the two classes have the same service
rate, but diﬀer for their arrival rates, their rewards, and their unit costs for being in the
system. We ﬁrst study the model where customers aim at maximizing their payoﬀ(given
what the other customers do). To do so, when they arrive at the queue, they can decide
whether to join it or to balk. At any given time, if they are in the queue, they can also
decide to renege.
We will prove existence of a unique equilibrium where A-customers use a threshold strat-
egy to decide whether to join the queue, and they never renege. They are oblivious to the
presence of B-customers, so they adopt a threshold strategy on the number of A-customers
in the queue, and the threshold is based exclusively on the parameters of their own class.
B-customers adopt a threshold strategy too, but the threshold is on the total number of
customers in the queue, and it depends also on the arrival rate of A-customers.
We then move to considering the social optimum that can be achieved by a planner
who receives the same reward as the served customers and pays the same waiting costs.
Despite this apparent alignment between the customers’ and the planner’s interests, the
optimal strategy diﬀers from the equilibrium strategy, because it takes into consideration
the externalities that the presence of each customer in the queue creates. A planner who
has no priority constraints will favor the class with the higher ratio of reward over waiting
cost. This class is not necessarily class A.
Finally we study the social optimum when priority is enforced. We model this situation
with one planner for each class. Each planner wants to maximize the social welfare of the
class, without and social consideration for customers of the other class.
What makes our model simultaneously interesting and complicated to handle is the co-
existence of balking and reneging in the equilibrium strategy of B-customers. Finding these
equilibrium strategies involves the analysis of the probability of ruin in a bi-dimensional
random walk. The structure of the problem allows us to reconduct this model to a uni-
dimensional ruin problem.
2


--- Page 3 ---
1.3
Related Literature
In his seminal paper Naor (1969) introduced the ﬁrst model of strategic queueing theory. In
his M/M/1 FCFS model, upon arrival at a queue, customers decide whether to join it or
to balk. They make their decision based on the parameters of the M/M/1 model, on the
reward that they receive when served, and on the cost they pay for each unit of time they
stay in the system. This deﬁnes a dynamic game. Naor proved that, if customers make their
decisions according to a selﬁsh criterion, i.e., if they play a Nash equilibrium of the above
game, the outcome is socially suboptimal, that is, a social planner could achieve a better
outcome.
Hassin (1985) proved that eﬃciency can be achieved at equilibrium, by using a diﬀerent
regime, e.g., last-come ﬁst-served (LCFS). Other regimes that achieve social optimality in
equilibrium were studied, among others, by Wang (2016), Haviv and Oz (2016, 2018), Che
and Tercieux (2024), Scarsini and Shmaya (2024b,a). Related issues concerning learning and
strategic experimentation were considered, among others, by Cripps and Thomas (2019),
Margaria (2024). In all these papers customers are assumed to be homogeneous and no
priority is allowed.
Several papers studied queuing models with priority, where customers can pay to achieve
a higher priority. Adiri and Yechiali (1974) considered a model where there are diﬀerent pri-
ority classes and customers can pay a fee to be a member of one of these classes. This model
was re-examined by Hassin and Haviv (1997), who showed that multiplicity of equilibria can
occur. Hassin and Haviv (2006) considered an M/M/1 model with two classes of customers,
where the queue is unobservable and customers pay a fee (diﬀerent for each class) to enter
the system. The server knows the customers’ classes and can set the fees. Haviv and Ravner
(2016) studied a strategic bidding system where customer bid for priority in a M/G/1 queue.
Chen and Kulkarni (2006, 2007) studied an M/M/1 queueing model with two priority
classes where customers can decide whether to join a queue or balk. Reneging is not allowed.
They compare the equilibrium outcome, the social optimum, and an intermediate situation
where each priority class has a social planner who care only about the welfare of customers
in that class. A similar model in the M/G/1 framework was considered by Xu et al. (2016,
2019). The model in Afèche and Sarhangian (2015) is the closest to the one in our paper.
They considered an M/M/1 FCFS queueing regime with two priority classes where customers
are assigned to a class, which they cannot choose. In the ﬁrst part of their paper, they
studied the customers’ equilibrium behavior, focusing on low-priority customers, given that
high-priority customers use a threshold strategy, where the threshold is as in Naor (1969).
They parametrized the state in terms of the total number of customers and the number of
low-priority customers in the system. This parametrization is diﬀerent from our, but totally
equivalent.
The expression that we obtain for the equilibrium threshold is simpler than
the one in Afèche and Sarhangian (2015). In the second part of their paper, these authors
studied the role of pricing to control customers’ balking and reneging.
The reader is referred to Hassin and Haviv (2003) for an extensive treatment of strategic
queueing and to Hassin (2016) for a detailed review of relevant contributions in the ﬁeld.
Some papers considered classical non-strategic queueing models with priority classes. For
3


--- Page 4 ---
instance, Wang et al. (2015) analyzed a M/M/c queueing model with two priority classes in
a non-strategic framework. Their analysis requires the reduction of a bi-dimensional Markov
chain to a uni-dimensional Markov chain. This is similar to what we have in our paper. In
the same framework, Rastpour et al. (2022) developed algorithms for multiserver queueing
systems with two customer classes, preemptive priorities, and reneging. To do this they used
quasi-birth-death process.
1.4
Organization of the Paper
The general model is described in Section 2. Section 3 deals with a variation of the model
where only B-customers act strategically. The fully strategic model, where both types of
customers act strategically is studied in Section 4. Section 5 considers the strategy of a
globally optimizing planner. Section 6 focuses on the case of two planners, one for each type
of customers.
1.5
Notation
Given a real number x, the symbol ⌊x⌋indicates the greatest integer n such that n ≤x.
The notation X ∼F means that the random variable X is distributed according to the
distribution function F.
2
The Model
Before dealing with our priority-class model, we recall the classical contributions concerning
strategic balking and reneging in an M/M/1 queueing model with homogeneous customers.
2.1
Homogeneous Customers
Naor (1969) studied an M/M/1 FCFS queueing model with reward for service and waiting
costs. More speciﬁcally:
• Customers arrive according to a Poisson process with parameter λ.
• Service times are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) according to an expo-
nential distribution with parameter µ
• Each customer receives a reward R upon service completion and incurs a cost C for
each unit of time spent in the system.
• Upon arrival at the queue each customer decides whether to join it or to balk.
• A customer who joins the queue receives a payoﬀ
R −CW,
(2.1)
where W is the random time spent in the system by this customer.
4


--- Page 5 ---
• A customer who balks receives a payoﬀequal to zero.
• A customer who balks will never come back to the queue.
Customer Chantal joins queue with n customers if and only if her expected payoﬀis
nonnegative, i.e.,
R −(n + 1)C
µ ≥0.
(2.2)
As a consequence, the integer
ne :=
Rµ
C

(2.3)
is such that
R −neC
µ ≥0
and
R −(ne + 1)C
µ < 0.
(2.4)
Therefore, the threshold strategy according to which a customer who observes ne or more
customers in the queue balks is an equilibrium strategy.
Naor (1969) considered a social planner who receives a reward R for each completed
service and pays a cost C for each time unit a customer spends in the queue.
Despite
this apparent interest alignment of the customers and the planner, the optimum strategy
is still a threshold strategy, but with a lower threshold. That is, the optimum threshold is
nopt = ⌊x∗⌋, where x∗is the unique solution of
Rµ
C = x(1 −ρ) −ρ(1 −ρx)
(1 −ρ)2
,
(2.5)
with ρ := λ/µ. The fact that nopt ≤ne is due to the fact that, selﬁshly behaving customers,
when they decide whether to join the queue or balk, do not take into account the generate
negative externalities they generate on the customers who will join the queue in the future.
This implies that they join the queue more than what is socially optimal.
2.2
Priority Model
We now consider an M/M/1 observable queuing system where customers can be of two types
A and B. Customers of type A have priority over customers of type B. So an A-customer
who arrives at the queue overtakes all the existing B-customers, even if a B-customer is
currently being served. Once the priority is established, the system follows a FCFS regime.
As previously mentioned, upon arrival each customer can observe the current state of the
system. Each customer of type θ ∈{A, B} incurs in a cost Cθ for each unit of time spent in
the system, and receives a reward Rθ upon service completion. The model can be formalized
as follows:
(i) A stream of customers A arrives according to a Poisson process with parameter λA.
(ii) A stream of customers B arrives according to a Poisson process with parameter λB.
5


--- Page 6 ---
(iii) The two Poisson processes are independent.
(iv) The service times of all customers are i.i.d. random variables having a exponential
distribution with parameter µ.
Moreover, for θ ∈{A, B} the symbol ρθ := λθ/µ denotes the utilization factor for θ-
customers. To avoid trivial situations, we assume that Rθµ ≥Cθ. If this inequality failed to
be true, the system would remain permanently idle.
B
Poisson(λB)
A
Poisson(λA)
B
B
B
A
A
A
Exp(µ)
Server
All parameters in the model are common knowledge and the queue is observable. When
customers arrive, they decide whether to join the queue or balk. Moreover, when they are
in the queue, they can renege, before getting served. However, only B-customers renege
because, unlike A-customers, their position in the queue can get worse with the arrival of
higher-priority customers.
2.3
Random Walks
We now introduce some notation and some stochastic tools that will be used in the sequel
to study the equilibrium and optimum of this model.
Deﬁnition 2.1. Consider a B-customer (Bridget) who has nA A-customers and nB B-
customers ahead of her.
We call P(nA, nB) the probability that Bridget is served, and
E(nA, nB) the expected total time that Bridget spends in the system.
Note that P(nA, nB) and E(nA, nB) depend on the the values (nA, nB), the queueing
system parameters, and the customers’ strategies.
Given a B-customer in the queue, consider the birth-and-death process (Yt)t≥0 that counts
the number of people in the system who are ahead of this customer at any given time t ≥0.
This process increases by 1 when an A-customer joins the queue, and decreases by 1 when a
service gets completed.
Let τ0 = 0 and, for every k ≥1, deﬁne
τk = inf{t > τk−1: at time t either a A-customer arrives or a service is completed}. (2.6)
Then, for every k > 0, τk −τk−1 ∼Exp(λA + µ), which implies
E(τk −τk−1) = 1/(λA + µ).
(2.7)
6


--- Page 7 ---
We let Xk := Yτk be the number of customers in the system at time τk. The process
(Xk)k∈N is a random walk with state space N such that X0 = n and
Xk+1 =





Xk + 1
with probability λA/(λA + µ),
Xk −1
with probability µ/(λA + µ), if Xk > 0,
Xk
with probability µ/(λA + µ), if Xk = 0.
(2.8)
The following graph pictorially represents the above transition probabilities.

3
Semi-Strategic Model
To better understand the fully strategic model of the following sections, it is convenient
to start our analysis with a “semi-strategic” regime where only B-customers are strategic,
whereas A-customers always join the queue and never renege. In this section, to guarantee
stability of the system, we assume that µ > λA. At any given time, a B-customer, knowing
the state of the queue, decides to join the queue (or to remain in it), if and only if the
expected future payoﬀof this customer is non-negative. The state of the queue is the pair
(nA, nB), representing the number of customers of type A and B who are already in the
system, respectively. Let (nA, nB) be the state of the system and let Bernard be the last
B-customer in the queue.
Bernard can be served only when all the customers ahead of
him have been served. The number of these customers increases with each arrival of a new
A-customer. Call ϕB(nA, nB) be the expected payoﬀfor Bernard, when he is in position
n = nA + nB, and would renege when in position n + 1. Then ϕB(nA, nB) can be written as
follows:
ϕB(nA, nB) = RBP(nA, nB −1) −CBE(nA, nB −1).
(3.1)
However, since A-customers are not strategic and the service regime is FCFS, the only
relevant information for Bernard is the total number of customers n = nA + nB in the
system. Hence ϕB(nA, nB) depends only on n and
ϕB(nA, nB) := ψB(n) = RBPn −CBEn,
(3.2)
where Pn and En are the probability of being served and the expected time spent in the
system by Bernard when he is in position n.
Lemma 3.1. For n ∈N,
ψB(n) = RB
1 −ρA
1 −ρn+1
A
−
CB
µ(1 −ρA)
n(1 −ρA) −ρA(1 −ρn
A)


.
(3.3)

--- Page 8 ---
Proof. Consider (3.2). We now compute the probability Pn that Bernard (in position n)
is served before reneging (position n + 1). We can interpret Pn as the ruin probability in
the gambler ruin problem where the initial position is n, the goal is n + 1 and the winning
probability in each round equals λA/(µ + λA). Then, by Theorem B.1,
Pn = 1 −ρA
1 −ρn+1
A
.
(3.4)
The total time En, spent in the system by Bernard when he is in position n corresponds
to the expected number of rounds until the gambler is either ruined or reaches her goal.
Then by Theorem B.2,
En =

3.1
Equilibrium Strategy
In the following theorem, we characterize the equilibrium strategy for a B-customer in the
semi-strategic setup.
Theorem 3.2. Let n ∈N be the position in the queue of a B-customer. The optimal strategy
for this B-customer is a threshold strategy, and the threshold Meq
B is the ﬂoor of the unique
solution of the following equation:
RBµ
CB
= n(1 −ρA) −ρA(1 −ρn

(1 −ρA)2
.
(3.5)
Proof. The threshold is the largest n for which ψB(n) is nonnegative. By equation (3.3), the
threshold is the largest n such that
RB

(3.6)

(1 −ρA)2

(3.7)
8


--- Page 9 ---
If we let γ(n) denote the right hand side of (3.7), then, the function γ( · ) is unbounded and
increasing, with γ(0) = 0, so there exists a unique value Meq
B such that
γ(Meq

≤γ(Meq

(3.8)
and Meq

The proofs of Lemma 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 resemble the proof of Hassin and Haviv
(2003, lemma 2.4), which deals with the social optimum threshold in the Naor model. In
fact, equations (2.5) and (3.5) diﬀer only in the choice of rewards and costs. This is not
surprising because, for a B-customer, the semi-strategical priority regime is equivalent to a
single-class LCFS regime with arrival rate λA.
4
Fully-Strategic Model
We consider now a fully-strategic queueing regime where all customers act strategically,
independently of their type. Since A-customers have priority over B-customers, their be-
havior is not inﬂuenced by the arrival of B-customers. Therefore, the equilibrium behavior
of A-customers is the same as in Naor (1969), i.e., they use a threshold strategy, where the
threshold is given by
MA =
RAµ
CA

.
(4.1)
The threshold MA sets an upper bound on the number of A-customers in the system. In the
fully-strategic model the state is the pair (nA, nB), representing the number of customers of
type A and B who are already in the system, respectively. The behavior of B-customers
depends on the threshold strategy of A-customers. In the following sections we characterize
the equilibrium strategy of a B-customer.
4.1
Equilibrium
Consider a fully-strategic priority queueing system where (nA, nB) is the state of the system.
We note that if the ratio RB/CB is not too large, then the B-customers will leave the queue
before the A-customers reach their own equilibroium threshold. To identify the value of
RB/CB at which this occurs, we ﬁrst analyze the situation in which a new B-customer
(Bernard) arrives and the state of the system is (MA, 0), so there are no B-customers in
the queue, whereas the number of A-customers is maximum. Given that the situation for
Bernard can never become worse, if he decides to join the queue, he will remain in the system
until his service is completed. Therefore, Bernard will join the queue if and only if
RB −CBE(MA, 0) ≥0,
(4.2)
The inequality in (4.2) implies that, if RB/CB < E(MA, 0), then a B-customer in the queue
will abandon the system before it reaches the state nA = MA.
9


--- Page 10 ---
Theorem 4.1. Consider a fully-strategic priority queueing regime. Let (nA, nB) be the state
of the system.
If RB/CB < E(MA, 0), then the optimal strategy for a B-customer is a
threshold strategy on the total number of customers n = nA + nB. Moreover, the threshold is
⌊n∗⌋where n∗is the unique solution of the following equation
RBµ
CB
=











(n + 1)(1 −ρA) −ρA(1 −ρn+1
A
)
(1 −ρA)2
if ρA ̸= 1
(n + 1)(n + 2)
2
if ρA = 1.
(4.3)
The proof of Theorem 4.1 is similar to that of Theorem 3.2. In fact, Eq. (4.3) coincides
with Eq. (3.5), except that n is replaced by n+1. This is due to the fact that, in Theorem 4.1,
the new B-customer would enter position n+1, whereas in Theorem 3.2, Bernard was already
in the queue at position n.
Moreover, the threshold given by (4.3) is smaller than MA. This can be proved with the
aid of Lemma C.2, which shows that the condition RB/CB < E(MA, 0) can be written as
follows:
RBµ
CB
<




if ρA ̸= 1

2
if ρA = 1.
(4.4)
Now we focus on the case RB/CB ≥E(MA, 0).
Theorem 4.2. Consider a fully-strategic queueing system. Assume that (nA, nB) is the state
of the system, and RB/CB ≥E(MA, 0). A B-customer joins the queue if n = nA +nB < TB,
where
TB := MA + VB,
(4.5)
and
VB =


2

if ρA = 1.
(4.6)
Proof. If RB/CB ≥E(MA, 0), the ﬁrst B-customer always joins the queue. We now deter-
mine the maximum number of B-customers that will always join the queue. To answer this
question we consider the worst-case scenario, i.e., we assume that the state of the system is
(MA, nB), and prove that, if RB/CB ≥E(MA, 0), then a new B-customer joins the queue
whenever nB < VB.
10


--- Page 11 ---
If the state is (MA, nB), as time goes by, the situation for a new B-customer who joins
the queue can only improve; therefore this customer will join if
RB −CBE(MA, nB) ≥0.
(4.7)
By Lemma C.2, if ρA ̸= 1, then (4.7) is equivalent to
RB ≥CB

(4.11)

RB ≥CB
1
2µ[MA(MA + 1) + 2(nB + 1)(MA + 1)],
(4.12)
that is,
2RBµ

(4.13)
which is the same as
RBµ
CB(MA + 1) −MA
2
≥nB + 1.
(4.14)
Hence, if the state of the system is (MA, nB), then a B-customer joins the queue if
nB < VB, where VB is given by (4.6).
Combining the thresholds in Eqs. (4.1) and (4.6), we see that the number of customers
in the system cannot exceed MA + VB. We now show that this value represents the optimal
threshold strategy for a B-customer in a fully-strategic queueing model when RB/CB ≥
E(MA, 0).
11


--- Page 12 ---
Let now (nA, nB) be the state of the system. By (4.1) and (4.6), n = nA + nB cannot
be larger than TB. Moreover, if nA ≤MA, we proved that a B-customer joins the queue
whenever nB < VB, and in this case, n is smaller than TB.
We now consider the case
nB ≥VB. Since n < TB, the value nA must be smaller than MA; therefore a new B-customer
(Brianna) joins the queue if
RBP(nA, nB) −CBE(nA, nB) ≥0,
(4.15)
where P(nA, nB) is the probability that Brianna completes the service before reneging (po-
sition TB + 1), and E(nA, nB) is the expected total time spent in the system by Brianna,
when the state of the system is (nA, nB).
We can interpret P(nA, nB) as the ruin probability in the gambler ruin problem that we
now describe. We consider a random walk on N2 with the following transition probabilities:
for nA = 0,
(nA, nB) →(nA + 1, nB)
w.p.
λA
λA + µ,
(nA, nB) →(nA, nB −1)
w.p.
µ
λA + µ,
for 0 < nA < MA,
(nA, nB) →(nA + 1, nB)
w.p.
λA
λA + µ,
(nA, nB) →(nA −1, nB)
w.p.
µ
λA + µ,
for nA = MA,
(nA, nB) →(nA, nB)
w.p.
λA
λA + µ,
(nA, nB) →(nA −1, nB)
w.p.
µ
λA + µ.
(4.16)
The transitions are represented in Fig. 1. Notice that, even if the random walk is on N2, in
every state, a transition occurs with positive probability only to two adjacent states. So this
random walk is equivalent to a single dimensional random walk.
Once Brianna reaches the position (0, VB), she gets served with probability 1. Hence, by
Theorem B.1,
P(nA, nB) =











1 −ρTB−n
A
1 −ρMA+1
A
if ρA ̸= 1
TB −n
MA + 1
if ρA = 1.
(4.17)
The expected total time E(nA, nB) spent in the system by Brianna can be interpreted as
the expected time before the ﬁrst arrival either at (0, 0) or at (nA, TB −nA + 1). Then, it is
possible to decompose E(nA, nB) in the following way
E(nA, nB) = bE(nA, nB) + P(nA, nB)E(0, VB −1),
(4.18)
where bE(nA, nB) is the expected time of ﬁrst arrival in either (0, VB) or (nA, TB −nA + 1)
starting from position n + 1; whereas E(0, VB −1), is the expected waiting time spent by
Brianna’s in the system when there are (0, VB −1) customers ahead of her.

(4.22)
So, by (4.6), Brianna joins the queue if n + 1 ≤TB.

.

Deﬁne

.

Claim 4.3. The function n 7→G(n) is increasing for VB ≤n ≤TB −1.
Proof. See Appendix C.
Hence, if we take n = TB −1 we have
0 ≥MA
␀1 −ρMA+1
A

−(MA + 1)ρA
␀1 −ρMA


+ ρA(1 −ρMA
A ) −MA(1 −ρA)
µ(1 −ρA)2
= MA(1 −ρA) −ρA
␀1 −ρMA
A

+ ρA(1 −ρMA
A ) −MA(1 −ρA)
µ(1 −ρA)2
= 0.
(4.27)
Then, the Brianna joins whenever n < TB.
14


--- Page 15 ---
Remark 4.4. In the whole paper, queues are assumed to be observable. It is important to
clarify that if Abigail is an A-customer and Bernard is a B-customer, they can make their
equilibrium decision by observing just the other customers that are (potentially) ahead of
them, in the queue. That is, when Abigail arrives at the queue, all she needs to know is how
many A-customers are already in the queue. She does not case about B-customers, because
they will never be ahead of her. When Bernard arrives at the queue or, if he already is in the
queue, he can make his balking or reneging equilibrium decisions based on the total number
of customers ahead of him in the queue. He does not need to know their types.
5
Global Optimization
In the previous section we analyzed the equilibrium behavior of selﬁsh customers both in
semi-strategic and fully strategic frameworks. We now focus on socially optimum behavior.
We assume the existence of a social planner who obtains a reward RA for any completed
service of an A-customer, a reward RB for any completed service of a B-customer, and incurs
a cost CA for any unit of time spent in the system by an A-customer and a cost CB for any
unit of time spent in the system by a B-customer. The social planner has full discretion
and can decide whom to accept in the queue and whom to kick out, without any priority
constraints. It is clear that the social planner has an incentive to favor customers whose
reward-to-cost ratio Rθ/Cθ is higher. For instance, if RA/CA > RB/CB, then the social
planner will admit at most M∗
A = ⌊n∗
A⌋customers in the system, where n∗
A is the unique
solution of the equation
RAµ
CA
=











n∗
A(1 −ρA) −ρA(1 −ρ
n∗
A
A )
(1 −ρA)2
if ρA ̸= 1,
nA(nA + 1)
2
if ρA = 1.
(5.1)
If nA < M∗
A, the planner can accept a number of B-customers. Given nA, the optimum total
number of customers that can be admitted can be found by imposing a LCFS regime the
customers’ equilibrium behavior. This is the technique used by Hassin (1985) to determine
the social optimum in the model with a single class. The idea is that in a LCFS regime the
decision made by the last customer in the queue does not generate any externality on the
other customers. Therefore what is optimal for this customer is also socially optimal.
If we deﬁne ρ := (λA + λB)/µ, then the optimum threshold on nA + nB for the admission
of a B-customer is ⌊n∗⌋, where n∗is the unique solution of the equation
RBµ
CB
=











n∗(1 −ρ) −ρ(1 −ρn∗)
(1 −ρ)2
if ρ ̸= 1
n∗(n∗+ 1)
2
if ρ = 1.
(5.2)
15


--- Page 16 ---
The above result implies that, if nA < M∗
A, and a new A-customer arrives, whenever the total
number of customers (including the newcomer) exceeds ⌊n∗⌋, a B-customer will be forced to
renege.
6
Class Optimization
We consider now the case where priorities are binding even for social planners. We represent
this situation by assuming that there exist two social planners, one for each type. As in
the previous section, for θ in {A, B}, the θ-planner receives a reward Rθ for each completed
service of a θ-customer and incurs a cost Cθ for each unit of time a θ-customer spends in the
system.
First we analyze the optimal strategy for the A-planner (Anastasia-Paris).
Since A-
customers are not inﬂuenced by B-customers, this is the classical optimal control problem
of the Naor model. Thus, as seen in the previous section, Anastasia-Paris’s threshold is the
following:
Theorem 6.1. The optimal strategy for an A-planner is a threshold strategy and the thresh-
old is
M∗
A = ⌊n∗
A⌋,
(6.1)
where n∗
A is the unique solution of
RAµ
CA
=











n∗
A(1 −ρA) −ρA(1 −ρ
n∗
A
A )
(1 −ρA)2
if ρA ̸= 1,
nA(nA + 1)
2
if ρA = 1.
(6.2)
We now consider the optimal strategy for the B-planner (Belinda-Paris), who knows
the optimal strategy of Anastasia-Paris. Along the lines of Hassin (1985), it can be shown
that Belinda-Paris’s optimal strategy coincides with the equilibrium strategy in a LCFS
regime. However, in our case, the B-customers’ equilibrium strategy depends on M∗
A. If
RB/CB < RA/CA, then Belinda-Paris’s optimal strategy coincides with the strategy used
by the unique social planner. Hence, we focus on Belinda-Paris’s optimal strategy when
RB/CB ≥RA/CA. In this situation, Belinda-Paris may accept B-customers even if there
are M∗
A A-customers in the queue.
Thus, as already done for the equilibrium strategy,
we assume that the state is (nA, nB), that the last B-customer (Benjamin) is in position
n = nA + nB > M∗
A, and that he will renege when in position n + 1. Unlike the equilibrium
case, however, now Benjamin’s position can become worse even after the arrival of another
B-customer. Hence, to ﬁnd the optimal strategy we have to compute the largest values n
such that
RBP(nA, nB −1) −CBE(nA, nB −1) ≥0,
(6.3)
It is possible to compute P(nA, nB −1) and E(nA, nB −1) by describing the problem as
a random walk.
16


--- Page 17 ---
nA
nB
M∗
A
n
n −M∗
A
(0, 0)
λB
µ
λA
λA
µ
λB
λA
µ
λB
λA
µ
λB
λA
µ
λB
Figure 2: Random-walk representation of a B-planner strategy
As shown in Fig. 2, P(nA, nB −1) represents the probability of reaching the vertex
(0, 0) before exiting the trapezoid, starting from a point on the blue segment (nA, n −nA).
Analogously to the equilibrium case, to reach the point (0, 0) the random walk must pass
through the point (0, n −M∗
A). However, reaching this point does not guarantee service
because the regime is LCFS. Similarly, E(nA, nB −1) represents the expected time before
the random walk reaches (0, 0) or exits the trapezoid, starting from a point on the blue
segment. Again, calculating the expected time is more complicated than in the previous
sections, because, even after reaching the rectangle in the ﬁgure, the random walk may
return to the triangle before reaching the point (0, 0). Just as we observed in the equilibrium
case, it is possible to obtain P(nA, nB −1) and E(nA, nB −1) as the solution of a system of
equations, but in this case ﬁnding the solution in closed form is quite complicated.
For every (i, j) in the trapezoid in Fig. 2, we call η(i, j) the probability of reaching (0, 0)
before leaving the trapezoid. Then, we can write P(nA, nB −1) as follows:
P(nA, nB −1) =











1 −ρ
1 −ρM∗
A+1η(0, n −M∗
A)
if ρ ̸= 1
1
M∗
A + 1η(0, n −M∗
A)
if ρ = 1,
(6.4)

--- Page 18 ---
where (1−ρ)/(1−ρM∗
A+1) and 1/(M∗
A+1) are the probabilities of reaching (0, n−M∗
A) before
leaving the triangle, starting from the blue segment (nA, n−nA). Moreover, by Theorem C.1
η(0, n −M∗
A) is obtained by the following system of equations:
η(0, 0) = 1,

Finally, if we call κ(i, j) the expected waiting time before reaching (0, 0) or leaving the
trapezoid, starting from (i, j), we can compute E(nA, nB −1) as follows:
E(nA, nB −1) =

A −1)(M∗

A).
7
Conclusions and Open Problems
We considered a strategic model of a FCFS M/M/1 queue where customers are of one of
two types A, B and A-customers have priority over B-customers. Customers cannot pay to
change their type. Customers of diﬀerent types have diﬀerent arrival rates, diﬀerent rewards,
and diﬀerent waiting costs. All these parameters are assumed to be common knowledge, and
the queue is observable. We characterized the unique equilibrium as a threshold strategy
for each type of customers. We then studied the optimum strategy of a planner who is not
bound by priorities. We ﬁnally moved to the case of two planners, one for each type, who
have to respect the priority of A over B.
19


--- Page 20 ---
A natural way to extend our results would be to consider more than two priority types.
This will face some serious computational hurdles, some of which already appeared in Sec-
tion 6. The solution of the equilibrium problem with two types is obtained by resorting to
the analysis of a bi-dimensional random walk, which can be reduced to a uni-dimensional
random walk. The probability of ruin and expected time in the system for this random walk
is solvable. Already, when computing the social optimum with two planners, the dimension-
ality reduction of the random walk is not feasible. Therefore, the solution of the problem
involves the ﬁrst exit time from a trapezoid of a truly bi-dimensional random walk. When
computing the equilibrium for the model with more than two priority types, even after di-
mensionality reduction, we need to consider the probability ﬁrst exit from a complicated set
of a multi-dimensional random walk. We can write down the structure of the problem, but
the best we can achieve is a numerical approximation of its solution.
Appendix A. List of Symbols
A, B
priority classes
Cθ
unitary waiting cost for customers of type θ
E(nA, nB)
expected total time spent by a B-customer in the system when there are
(nA, nB) customers ahead of them, deﬁned in Deﬁnition 2.1
En
expected time spent in the system by a B-customer in position n
MA
Naor’s equilibrium threshold for A-customers
M∗
A
Naor’s optimum threshold for A-customers
Meq
B
equilibrium threshold in a semi-strategic system, introduced in Theorem 3.2
n
nA + nB
nA
number of A-customers
nB
number of B-customers
P(nA, nB)
probability of being served for a B-customer when there are (nA, nB) cus-
tomers ahead of them, deﬁned in Deﬁnition 2.1
Pn
probability that a B-customer in position n gets served
Rθ
reward for service completion for customers of type θ
TB
MA + VB, deﬁned in Eq. (4.5)
VB
introduced in Theorem 4.2
Xk
Yτk
Yt
birth-and-death process
η(i, j)
ruin probability in the Markov chain in Fig. 2 starting from (i, j)
θ
generic type, θ ∈{A, B}
µ
service rate
λθ
arrival rate for customers of type θ

Appendix B. Gambler’s Ruin
This section is based on Ethier (2010, chapter 7).
Consider a bet that pays one unit of money. Let p denote the probability of a win for
the gambler, and q = 1−p the probability of a loss. Deﬁning by Y the random variable that
represents the gambler proﬁt after a single bet, we have
Y =
(
1
with prob. p
−1
with prob. q.
Let (Yk)k≥1 be a sequence of R.V. with the same distribution of Y , that is a sequence of
independent bet. Then
Sn = Y1 + · · · + Yn
represents the gambler’s cumulative proﬁt, with S0 = 0. Consider now W and L in N, we
assume that the gambler stops betting as soon as he wins W units or loses L units. Hence,
the gambler stops to bet at the stopping time
T(−L, W) = inf{n ≥0 : Sn = −L or Sn = W}.
Then the gambler’s ruin and the gambler’s expected duration of play are the following.
Theorem B.1 (Ethier 2010, theorem 7.1.1).
P
␀ST(L,W ) = −L

=









1 −
( q
p)
L−1
( q
p)
L+W −1
if p ̸= q
W
L+W
if p = q
(B.1)
Theorem B.2 (Ethier 2010, theorem 7.1.4).
E[T(−L, W)] =









L+W
q−p

L
L+W −
( q
p)
L−1
( q
p)
L+W −1

if p ̸= q
L · W
if p = q
.
(B.2)
Finally, the following corollary represents the limit cases when L and W are equal to
inﬁnity.
21


--- Page 22 ---
Corollary B.3 (Ethier 2010, corollary 7.1.6). If p>q, then
E[T(−∞, W)] =
W
p −q.
(B.3)
If p<q, then
E[T(−L, ∞)] =
L
q −p.
(B.4)
Appendix C. Additional Proofs
In this section, we will present all the tools necessary to prove Theorem 4.2.
We begin
recalling a result from Markov chain regarding hitting times.
Theorem C.1 (Norris 1997, theorems 1.3.2 and 1.3.5). Let (Xn)n≥0 be a Markov chain on the
state space I and transition matrix P. For every J ⊂I deﬁne by HJ := inf{n ≥0 : Xn ∈J}
the hitting time in J. Moreover, for every i ∈I deﬁne ηi = Pi(HJ < ∞) and κi = Ei[HJ].
Then, (ηi)i∈I is the minimal nonnegative solution of the following linear system
(
ηi = 1
if i ∈J
ηi = P
j∈I Pijηj
if i ∈I\J,
(C.1)
and (κi)i∈I is the minimal nonnegative solution of the following linear system
(
κi = 0
if i ∈J
κi = 1 + P
j∈I Pijκj
if i ∈I\J
(C.2)
Let (NA, NB) be a random walk on N2 with NA ≤L and the following transition proba-
bilities:
for NA = 0,
(NA, NB) →(NA + 1, NB)
w.p.
λA
λA + µ,
(NA, NB) →(NA, NB −1)
w.p.
µ
λA + µ,
for 0 < NA < L,
(NA, NB) →(NA + 1, NB)
w.p.
λA
λA + µ,
(NA, NB) →(NA −1, NB)
w.p.
µ
λA + µ,
for NA = L,
(NA, NB) →(NA, NB)
w.p.
λA
λA + µ,
(NA, NB) →(NA −1, NB)
w.p.
µ
λA + µ.
The transitions are represented in Fig. 3. Notice that, given a starting point (nA, nB) the
random walk is conﬁned to a rectangle with a base of L and a height of nB. Moreover, even
if the random walk is on N2, in every state, a transition occurs with positive probability
only to two adjacent states. In particular, it moves horizontally as long as NA > 0, moves
downward vertically only when NA = 0, and never moves upward.

NB
L
nB
(0, 0)

λA

µ
λA
µ
λA
µ
Figure 3: Random-walk in a rectangle
Lemma C.2. Consider the random walk in Fig. 3 with initial state (nA, nB). Then, letting
ρA := λA/µ, the expected time of reaching (0, 0) is

if ρA = 1.
(C.3)
The proof of Lemma C.2 requires the following results.
Lemma C.3. Let (Xk)k≥0 be a random walk on the state space {0, 1, . . . , L}, with initial
state X0 = nA, and transition probabilities given by Fig. 4. Let UnA be the expected time the
random walk takes to reach 0. Then, for 0 ≤nA ≤L, we have

Proof of Lemma C.2. We begin noting that:
H(nA, nB) = H(nA, nB −1) + H(0, 1)
= H(nA, 0) + nBH(0, 1)
= UnA + nBH(0, 1).
(C.16)
Then, by Lemma C.3 we have
UnA =
(
1
µ(1−ρA)
h

if ρA ̸= 1,
nA
2µ (2L + 1 −nA)
if ρA = 1.
(C.17)
To compute H(0, 1) we need to consider two cases: either with probability µ/(µ + λA)
NB decreases by 1, or NA increases by 1 with probability λA/(µ + λA)). Then,
H(0, 1) =

(C.23)

Now, with the change of variable x = ρTB−n
A
, the result follows from the following inequality:
1 −x(1 −ln x) ≥0,
for x > 0.

Funding
Maurizio D’Andrea and Marco Scarsini are members of GNAMPA-INdAM. Their work was
partially supported by the MIUR PRIN 2022EKNE5K “Learning in markets and society.”

monopoly queues. Oper. Res., 22(5):1051–1066.

and pricing implications. Technical report, Columbia Business School Research Paper.

Queueing Networks, and Manufacturing Systems, pages 73–95. Springer, New York.

two-priority queues. Stoch. Models, 23(1):97–127.

Applications. Springer.

Kluwer Academic Publishers, Boston, MA.
